They don't like a sense of entitlement in a salesperson, so they like to be asked. Third, often when you really create a spectacular offer, it will engender some skepticism, which is one of the ways you know it's really great. Is if people are questioning how can you do that or why would you do that or what can't I see that must be wrong with this, which now that has to be addressed and dealt with. So very rarely is there such a spectacular offer that it will stand alone. You have to sell. They want to be romanced. They think we talk about it. People enjoy to buy. That's a key word there by the way, romance and selling. It really is. They want to be romanced. They want to be loved. They want appreciation shown both before, during and after the sale. And I think it has to happen and I think it's part of the process. The pros realize that. So if you took Bob Stupak's Irresistible offer, which I've shown a number of times. It's in the archives collection by the way. So his Irresistible offer for many years, the control for Vegas world, which is now the stratosphere that brought in all the money to build Vegas world from one floor to what it is, was, and I may now be a tick off, but I'll be close to correct, three nights, two days, deluxe room in the VIP tower, which was the only tower. All the booze you can drink, whether you're gambling or in the lounge. So there was no tabs in the lounge. You drink for free. A few meals, some premiums. So you got some fuzzy dice and stuff when you got there. And $600 to gamble with for $297. So everybody could do that math. Now the 600 was match play, but even with match play, it was 300. So everybody could do that math. So the person of the school of, if the offer is irresistible enough, we don't need any salesmanship for it. You could buy a full page ad in a travel magazine, a casino magazine, and just state it the way I just stated it and people would buy it. Not of course the case. In StuPak's case, if you find the ad, you'll find it full of copy. Not just about the offer, but romancing the idea of the experience you're going to have at Vegas World and you're at the coolest place and all that. If you've got a direct mail piece, you've got an eight page sales letter and a brochure and you've got some flattery, some positioning about having a high roller experience and Vegas discriminates really against the ordinary working folk who I like. So a whole lot of salesmanship went into this offer that on its face, you should only have to state it and people should throw money at you. And so if that's not the case with that, I defy you to build one that has better economics. Got you. Got you. All right, let's go to what seems to be, you would think it would be a fundamental, but man, every time I do this in a workshop, people still get this wrong. The difference between features and benefits. Let's go as fundamental as we can get here. What's the difference between a feature and a benefit and then we'll go to a second question about this. I like to get this wiped off the map once and for all this problem because it's amazing that people still don't get this. And it doesn't matter where the salesperson is or what they're selling, they always struggle with this. And I think in its simplest form, a feature is an attribute. It's a spec. It is something. It's tangible. If you could use that word, where a benefit is lifts them up. It's the end result. If you think about pain and pleasure, we move them towards the benefit of the product or we move them away from the pain of not having the product. And I think people get confused because it's easy for us to think about features and it makes it, it's quick, right? And it goes back to laziness and selling where they won't use scripts and they won't do the homework. And so we're just going to go in and we're going to throw the marbles. We're going to throw all the features on the table to them. And if you're a car salesman, we're going to talk about how fast the engine is and zero to 16 and all that has created the benefits of having the car. And we haven't done the work on the upfront.
